title: STAT-515
text:
Welcome to STAT-515! This site contains course materials, projects, and statistical analyses. The Midterm project includes identifying an existign visualization from the web or another published source and creating several redesigned versions of that same visualization. The goal is to offer critique and improve an existing visual with several redesign images.


Click on “Projects” in the navigation menu above to view all course projects.

title: Midterm Redesign Code Overview
text:
This page provides a brief overview of the code used to generate the Gold Reserves mid term project.
We first load libraries and packages that will be needed.

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.2
✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.1.0     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors


We created a csv file by copying the data found in the USGS January 2025 report on page 83. This was feed into Claude, an AI tool that has gained recent popularity which made it simple to generate a proper csv file. As you can see, the original chart includes commas as thousands separators and superscript markers in the Reserves column and elsewhere, which makes it difficult to import cleanly into a data frame with read_csv() in R.



Source data taken directly from the USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025


After creating the clean data.csv referenced in the original source image, the csv file loads into a data frame with no issues.

# Read the csv into a data frame then build plots by layering ggplot() + geom_*() (histogram, bar, box, ect.) use coord_flip() for horizontal versions.

# Begin by loading the data from the csv file. 
df <- read_csv("../data/data.csv")

Rows: 19 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (1): Country
dbl (3): Production_2023, Production_2024, Reserves

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

# head(df)   # Not as useful as the glimpse() function below...

# This will give you the column names and types so you know what to plot.
glimpse(df)

Rows: 19
Columns: 4
$ Country         <chr> "United States", "Australia", "Brazil", "Burkina Faso"…
$ Production_2023 <dbl> 170, 296, 71, 57, 198, 375, 61, 126, 100, 133, 67, 127…
$ Production_2024 <dbl> 160, 290, 70, 60, 200, 380, 60, 130, 100, 130, 70, 130…
$ Reserves        <dbl> 3000, 12000, 2400, NA, 3200, 3100, 700, 1000, 3600, 23…


Analysis of the data shows that we have at least one NA value that should be removed; it also contains a mix of categorical values (Countries) and a few numeric measures. This leads well to bar-like ranking and change over time charts. Lets try to create a single year reserve chart using either bar

title: Midterm Redesign Code Overview
text:
Data preparation
The data set has at least one NA values that should be removed.

print(df)

# A tibble: 19 × 4
   Country               Production_2023 Production_2024 Reserves
   <chr>                           <dbl>           <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 United States                     170             160     3000
 2 Australia                         296             290    12000
 3 Brazil                             71              70     2400
 4 Burkina Faso                       57              60       NA
 5 Canada                            198             200     3200
 6 China                             375             380     3100
 7 Colombia                           61              60      700
 8 Ghana                             126             130     1000
 9 Indonesia                         100             100     3600
10 Kazakhstan                        133             130     2300
11 Mali                               67              70      800
12 Mexico                            127             130     1400
13 Peru                              100             100     2500
14 Russia                            313             310    12000
15 South Africa                      104             100     5000
16 Tanzania                           55              60      400
17 Uzbekistan                        120             120     1800
18 Other countries                   777             780     9200
19 World total (rounded)            3250            3300    64000


Creating some basic histograms using the data frame.

# Histogram
ggplot(df, aes(x = Reserves)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, color = "salmon", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Reserves Distribution",
    x = "Country",
    y = "Reserves"
  ) +
  theme_dark()

Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).








# Histogram
df %>% 
  mutate(!is.na(Reserves)) %>%
  filter(!Country == "World total (rounded)") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Reserves, fill = Reserves)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 40, color = "white", fill = "steelblue") +
    labs(
      title = "Reserves distribution",
      x = "Country",
      y = "Reserves"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()

Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).








## Bar chart

ggplot(df, aes(x = Country)) + 
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar chart",
    x = "Country",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_dark()








Comments on increasing reserves in this time frame…perhaps due to rising gold prices, expected tariffs ect.

Data cleaning
Remove NA values and also the last row titled “World total”.

# Create a filtered tibble removing NA and "World total...."
df_clean = df %>%
  filter(
    !Country == "World total (rounded)",
    !is.na(Reserves)
  )
df_clean

# A tibble: 17 × 4
   Country         Production_2023 Production_2024 Reserves
   <chr>                     <dbl>           <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 United States               170             160     3000
 2 Australia                   296             290    12000
 3 Brazil                       71              70     2400
 4 Canada                      198             200     3200
 5 China                       375             380     3100
 6 Colombia                     61              60      700
 7 Ghana                       126             130     1000
 8 Indonesia                   100             100     3600
 9 Kazakhstan                  133             130     2300
10 Mali                         67              70      800
11 Mexico                      127             130     1400
12 Peru                        100             100     2500
13 Russia                      313             310    12000
14 South Africa                104             100     5000
15 Tanzania                     55              60      400
16 Uzbekistan                  120             120     1800
17 Other countries             777             780     9200

# Print the clean data frame renamed as reserves
# print(df_clean)
glimpse(df_clean)

Rows: 17
Columns: 4
$ Country         <chr> "United States", "Australia", "Brazil", "Canada", "Chi…
$ Production_2023 <dbl> 170, 296, 71, 198, 375, 61, 126, 100, 133, 67, 127, 10…
$ Production_2024 <dbl> 160, 290, 70, 200, 380, 60, 130, 100, 130, 70, 130, 10…
$ Reserves        <dbl> 3000, 12000, 2400, 3200, 3100, 700, 1000, 3600, 2300, …

title: Midterm Redesign Code Overview
text:
Looking for trends
We can also visualize how mine production changed from 2023 to 2024 for each country.

# Mutate the original data frame with out reassigning it, simply pass the mutated object directly into the ggplot.
# begin with original data, filter 'na' values, create new column "Change_23_24"
# Create vertical histogram by using coord_flip, 

df %>%
  filter(!is.na(Production_2023), !is.na(Production_2024)) %>%
  mutate(Change_23_24 = Production_2024 - Production_2023) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Country, Change_23_24),
             y = Change_23_24,
             fill = Change_23_24 > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "darkgreen", "FALSE" = "firebrick"),
    name   = "Change",
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Decrease or no change", "TRUE" = "Increase")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in gold mine production, 2023 to 2024",
    x = "Country",
    y = "Change in mine production (metric tons)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




Change in gold mine production from 2023 to 2024 by country (metric tons).




That looks good, however, the extreme value “World total (rounded)” is throwing off the scale. We should remove this outlier.

title: Midterm Redesign Code Overview
text:
Remove outliers
After removing the World Total, the image is much easier to see trends.

# Mutate the original data frame with out reassigning it, simply pass the mutated object directly into the ggplot.
# begin with original data, filter 'na' values, create new column "Change_23_24"
# Create vertical histogram by using coord_flip, 

df %>%
  filter(!is.na(Production_2023), !is.na(Production_2024)) %>%
  mutate(Change_23_24 = Production_2024 - Production_2023) %>%
  filter(
    !Country == "World total (rounded)",
    !is.na(Reserves)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Country, Change_23_24),
             y = Change_23_24,
             fill = Change_23_24 > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "darkgreen", "FALSE" = "firebrick"),
    name   = "Change",
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Decrease or no change", "TRUE" = "Increase")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in gold mine production, 2023 to 2024",
    x = "Country",
    y = "Change in mine production (metric tons)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




Change in gold mine production from 2023 to 2024 by country (metric tons).





Redesigning the original image
We will begin by introducing a foundation image using ggplot2 and then incrementally adding layers to it. Read the csv into a data frame then build plots by layering ggplot() + geom_*() (histogram, bar, box, ect.) use coord_flip() for horizontal versions.

title: Adding a new project
text:
We will leave this around in the case that any new projects are added in the future.


add some image, redesign, and render it with “Render Website”



Add a new .qmd file to the projects directory
Make adjustments to the file

Be sure to include any images that are linked in the qmd file

Edit the file named “_quarto.yml”

website.navbar.left.menu Include the link and test to display.

Render the website in R-studio
Push changes to github
See changes in the github hosted site
test

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
The dynamics of the global gold market is governed by not only the above-ground stock but also the geographic distribution of reserves in the ground which are yet to be extracted. According to recent estimates from the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS), the amount of proven gold reserves total on the order of tens of thousands metric tons worldwide with substantial concentrations among just a limited number of producers.
In a 2025 visualization published by Visual Capitalist, nations are ranked by their un-mined gold reserves using the data from the USGS estimate in order to illuminate the perspective geography of future extraction. While the original image is visually engaging and communicates the basic ranking of nations, the design makes it difficult to extract precise quantitative comparisons. It falls short of an analytical instrument and is not sophisticated enough to provide the level of accuracy required for through analysis.
In this project, we will address those limitations through systematic reconstruction of the original design using data from the Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025 published by the USGS, we source the exact data cited in the original graphic.

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
The dynamics of the global gold market is governed by not only the above-ground stock but also the geographic distribution of reserves in the ground which are yet to be extracted. According to recent estimates from the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS), the amount of proven gold reserves total on the order of tens of thousands metric tons worldwide with substantial concentrations among just a limited number of producers.
In a 2025 visualization published by Visual Capitalist, nations are ranked by their un-mined gold reserves using the data from the USGS estimate in order to illuminate the perspective geography of future extraction. While the original image is visually engaging and communicates the basic ranking of nations, the design makes it difficult to extract precise quantitative comparisons. It falls short of an analytical instrument and is not sophisticated enough to provide the level of accuracy required for through analysis.
In this project, we will address those limitations through systematic reconstruction of the original design using data from the Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025 published by the USGS, we source the exact data cited in the original graphic.

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
The image selection
For this project I drew inspiration from my fiancee Emily’s home office, where she conducts research on economic markets. As a professional economist she frequently works with data-rich visual resources. Among these, one particularly influential source was Elements, a platform that uses data visualization to explore the relationship between global trends and the natural resources behind them.



Emily’s home office

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
Overview
This project is a recreation of the “Unmined Reserves of Gold” info graphic (shown below) using ggplot2. The original shows estimated gold reserves by country displayed in metric tons and valued in USD determined by a gold price factor of $4,362 per troy ounce.

Original image design
In the original image titled, “Ranked: Unmined Gold Reserves by Country”, the graphic presents a detailed comparison of countries’ unmined gold reserves, ranked by estimated weight. The figure reports reserves in metric tons and also provides an inferred monetary value for these reserves, calculated using a pegged gold price of $4,362 per unit.

Original image:



Source image: https://elements.visualcapitalist.com/ranked-unmined-gold-reserves-by-country-2025/





Data source
As you can see in the original graphic, the data source references the U.S Geological Survey Janurary 2025. In the reports table of content, a section reporting gold is found on page 83 directly after germanium. On the following page, a table is displayed which includes the data used for this project. I copied the data from the report and created a file named data.csv which contains the following columns for the following:
country name, production for both years 2023, 2024, and total reserves



Original data source usgs.gov, Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
Observations
Some observations about the original graphic, data, likely conclusions that can be inferred. One can see how this would lend if coupled with other sources of related information, for example: - central bank gold purchases per year (or change year over year) - gold reserves held by country

The data
Errors found in the data: Indonesia reports reserves of 3.6K however the image displays 3.8K.
Also note that the original info graphic includes data for a single year however the data we pulled from the source, Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025, includes production for years 2023-24 as well as the reserves.

title: Ranked Unmined Gold Reserves by Country
text:
Data preparation
The data included 19 samples of 4 measurements. As you can see, the data contains NA values we will need to remove, and also last row, “World total” which is not included in the original image.

The Data

title: About
text:
I am Jonathan, a Master’s student at George Mason University in the Department of Statistics. I am interested in data science and statistics as it relates to machine learning and artificial intelligence.
I received a Bachelor’s degree in Applied Physics from Armstrong Atlantic State University in 2014.
My research interests include:

Semiconductor fabrication
Experimental design
Computer vision

I also enjoy hiking, biking, gardening, and I have a fruit orchard in my front yard.
